exemplar.title: Modeling the velocity of a skydiver to find minimum jump height and parachute size

detailedExemplarData.title: Modeling the velocity of a skydiver to find minimum jump height and parachute size

subject.title: Mathematics Analysis and Approaches (AA)
subject.description:
**IB Mathematics Analysis and Approaches** is a **Group 5** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **number and algebra, functions, geometry and trigonometry, statistics and probability, and calculus**, with a strong focus on **theoretical understanding, formal mathematical reasoning, and analytical problem-solving**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2021**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| Assessment | Format | SL | HL | Weighting |
|------------|--------|----|----|-----------|
| **Paper 1** | Non-calculator written exam | 90 minutes | 120 minutes | 40% |
| **Paper 2** | Calculator allowed written exam | 90 minutes | 120 minutes | 40% |
| **Paper 3** | Calculator allowed problem-solving | N/A | 60 minutes | 20% (HL only) |
| **Mathematical Exploration** | Internal Assessment | 20 hours | 20 hours | 20% |

strengths: Clear title and research question set the context and aim effectively
Recognizable overall structure with titled sections, tables, figures and conclusion
Most material relates directly to modelling velocity and parachute parameters
weaknesses: Several lengthy digressions reduce conciseness and interrupt flow
Abrupt transitions between sections (e.g., freefall to parachute phase)
Repetition of algebraic work in subâsections and missing inâtext citations/axis labels
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The exploration is coherent and logically developed in places but hampered by disjointed narrative and digressions. Organization is adequate with clear headings and a conclusion, yet subâsection repetition and missing academic conventions prevent a higher band. Overall, it meets Band 2 criteria for presentation.

strengths: Effective use of multiple representations: tables, analytical equations and DESMOS graphs
Generally consistent and mostly accurate mathematical language and notation
Logical progression of arguments with piecewise velocity graph clearly integrating phases
weaknesses: Units often misâformatted (e.g., âms^{-1}â instead of âm s^{-1}â or âm s^{-2}â)
Key algebraic steps are omitted (partialâfractions, terminal velocity derivation)
Inconsistent variable conventions and occasional signâconvention ambiguity
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The submission employs a variety of wellâlabelled representations and uses mathematical language with reasonable consistency. Clarity and logical structure are good, though precision lapses and omitted steps prevent an excellent classification, placing this criterion at Band 3.

strengths: Personal motivation is clear through firstâperson reflection on skydiving experience
Modelâbuilding choices (99% terminal velocity threshold, headâfirst variant) show independent thinking
The authorâs own mass and âthrillâ criteria provide a personal stamp on the exploration
weaknesses: Many modelling steps follow standard physics treatment with limited innovation
Creativity and initiative are moderate rather than exceptional
Some decisions lack further personal justification or deeper exploration
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: There is significant evidence of independent thinking and a clear personal approach, but much of the methodology mirrors textbook dragâforce models. Creativity is present but not sustained at a high level, warranting a Band 2 score.

strengths: Conclusion identifies key model limitations and suggests future extensions
Some meaningful reflection on constant density assumption and deployment delay
weaknesses: Critical analysis is mostly descriptive without deep examination of mathematical validity
Evaluation of outcomes lacks quantitative crossâchecks or external data comparisons
Reflection on safety implications and model robustness is limited
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: While the conclusion shows some meaningful reflection on limitations, the analysis remains basic and the evaluation of results is limited. The work falls into Band 1 for reflection, demonstrating only initial reflective engagement.

strengths: Calculusâbased drag modelling and differential equation derivations are appropriate and relevant
Correct use of substitution to simplify expressions and sound application of Newtonâs second law
Key results (terminal velocity, height) largely correct with proper significantâfigure practice
weaknesses: Minor algebraic grouping errors and sign slips in differential equation manipulation
Omission of intermediate steps in integration leads to reduced mathematical rigor
Repetition of work and occasional arithmetic/unit inconsistencies
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The mathematics used is fully commensurate with SL level and clearly relevant to the aim. Although understanding is good, minor errors and skipped steps prevent a higher band, placing it at the midârange Band 3 for Use of Mathematics.

strandSelections.reasoning: **Calculations are usually shown logically, but sign errors (factor of 2 in exponent, misuse of âxâ instead of variables) and skipped algebra steps reduce clarity.**  The reader can follow with effort; the exposition is not consistently clear.

**Key results (terminal velocity, height) are largely correct, but integration steps show minor algebraic inconsistencies and rounding errors.**  Understanding is good but not thorough, justifying the mid-band.

**Tables, analytical equations, and DESMOS graphs are all employed and generally support the discussion.**  Graphs are labelled (axes, key points) though scales are plain.  The variety is effective even if not âhighlyâ so.

**The argument generally proceeds in a sensible order (introduction â modelling â calculation â reflection), but sections often feel disjointed and some algebraic detours interrupt the narrative.**  Mathematical ideas can be followed, yet links between consecutive paragraphs are occasionally abrupt (e.g., sudden switch from belly-to-earth to head-first case).  Overall coherence is present but not sustained at a high level.

**Creativity is present in setting thresholds (5.01 m sâ»Â¹) and constructing a piece-wise model, yet solutions largely mirror standard drag-force treatment.**  Hence only âsomeâ initiative is evident.
**Analysis of results is mostly descriptive (âincrease is proportionalâ); mathematical validity of integrals is not critically examined.**  Basic, but not thoughtful, critical analysis.
**Outcomes (500 m vs 2 900 m) are compared with ârealismâ, yet no quantitative test or external data check is attempted.**  Evaluation is limited.

**The conclusion discusses limitations (constant Ï, instant deployment) and alternative improvements, showing meaningful reflection.**  Depth is adequate but not âsubstantialâ throughout the paper.

**Standard symbols (v, t, g, Ï, C_D) are used but units are sometimes omitted or mis-typed (ms^{-1} written as ms^{-1}).**  Long exponent strings are copied from DESMOS without proper typesetting.  The notation is acceptable but not consistently precise.

**The context (authorâs own mass, quest for âmaximum thrillâ) gives a clear personal stamp and the study extends to an alternative diving posture.**  This demonstrates a personal approach but not a highly innovative one.

**The exploration contains several lengthy digressions (e.g., side comments on âthrillâ or human g-tolerance) and algebra is sometimes reproduced twice.**  Nevertheless most material is relevant to determining velocity, parachute size and height, so irrelevance is not excessive.

**Calculus-based drag modelling, terminal velocity formulae, and definite integrals are clearly relevant to the aim and used appropriately.**  Work sometimes repeats or contains algebra slips, preventing a higher band.

**Deriving a differential-equation model and choosing 99 % terminal velocity shows personal decision-making beyond textbook methods.**  However, many steps follow standard physics sources and errors remain, so independent thinking is âsignificantâ rather than âoutstandingâ.

**There is a recognizable structure with titled sections, tables, figures and a conclusion.**  Headings guide the reader and a piece-wise graph neatly combines phases.  However, sub-sections occasionally repeat earlier content and the bibliography is not integrated with in-text citations, preventing a âexcellentâ classification.
**Differential equations, partial fractions and exponential models are fully commensurate with SL level and occasionally venture slightly beyond.**  Application is sound though not exemplary.

annotations.comment: The narrative includes digressions (roller coasters, thrill discussion) that reduce conciseness; focus strictly on relevant background to maintain flow.
Units in the equation for \(g=9.81\) are mis-formatted as âms^{-1}â; ensure correct notation \(\mathrm{m\,s^{-2}}\) for acceleration.
The derivation of \(v=gt\) from \(a=dv/dt\) is clearly presented and demonstrates sound use of integration.
Reference to â(Homer)â lacks in-text citation details; include full author and date to adhere to academic conventions.
The title and research question are clearly stated, immediately conveying the level, context, and aim of the exploration, aiding the readerâs understanding of scope.
Administrative detail âNo. of Pages: 20â is unnecessary; removing it will streamline the presentation and maintain focus on content.
The introduction uses first-person reflection on skydiving to demonstrate clear personal engagement and motivation for the topic.
The computed speed \(883\,\mathrm{m/s}\) from \(v=gt\) is physically impossible; this highlights the need to include drag but also suggests a unit or arithmetic check.
The variable table clearly lists mass, drag coefficient, and area, providing helpful context for subsequent modelling.
The link between the asymptotic behavior and the freefall model is clearly articulated and supports interpretation of the graph.
The derivation for solving \(v=0.99v_t\) lacks intermediate algebraic steps; show the full manipulation to clarify the method.
The piecewise velocity graph effectively integrates both phases, clearly indicating intersection at opening time.
When integrating for height, include evaluation of definite integrals step by step instead of skipping from set-up to result.
Rounded total height \(\approx500\,\mathrm{m}\) demonstrates correct significant-figure practice, linking calculation to real-world benchmarks.
Units in âms^{-1}â appear inconsistently; standardize to \(\mathrm{m\,s^{-1}}\) throughout to improve clarity.
The substitution \(z=\tfrac12\rho C_D A\) effectively reduces complexity; well applied to simplify subsequent equations.
When stating \(F_{net}=mg - zv^2\), clarify sign convention and direction of forces to avoid ambiguity.
Intermediate algebra steps are omitted when rearranging the terminal velocity formula; include these for full mathematical communication.
The derivation of the differential equation \(m\,dv/dt = mg - zv^2\) is correctly executed, showing understanding of Newtonâs second law.
The step \(dv/dt = (z/m)(g(m/z) - v^2)\) shows a grouping error; check algebra when factoring \(z/m\).
Partial-fractions decomposition omits explicit absolute-value handling; specify conditions on \(|v\pm\sqrt f|\) for rigorous justification.
Introduce and define the constant \(L=e^{2\sqrt f C}\) more explicitly; discuss its origin to strengthen mathematical communication.
Figures lack axis labels and units; adding clear labels will enhance readability and ensure precise interpretation.
Transition to parachuteâphase description is abrupt; add a linking sentence to improve the logical flow between sections.
In the head-first model, the exponent uses âxâ instead of âzâ; correct variable consistency to avoid confusion.
Discussion of the safety implications of prolonged deceleration time would deepen reflection on model validity.
The conclusion critically summarises limitations and suggests future extensions, showing thoughtful reflection on the investigationâs scope.
